title: St. Christopher, St. Theodosia, St. Amphianus & St. Aedesius the Brother-Martyrs
primary_saint: St. Christopher the Great Martyr, St. Theodosia the Martyr, St. Amphianus of Caesarea, and St. Aedesius his Brother
narrative:
On the second day of Miazia, the holy Church gathers four witnesses under the sign of bearing and being borne — the giant who bore Christ across the river, the young woman who bore witness at the gates of the city, and two brothers who bore each other's example toward martyrdom. Saint Christopher is among the most beloved and mythologised of the early martyrs, his historical core surrounded by a tradition that grew to legendary proportions but whose theological meaning is perfectly lucid: the great strong man who serves Christ by carrying travellers across a dangerous river, and who one night carries a child so heavy that the rivers runs with the weight of the world, and who discovers on the far bank that the child he carried was the one who carries all things. The Ethiopian tradition honours Christopher as a witness to the evangelical paradox that the greatest strength is expressed in service, and that the one who makes himself the bearer of others bears, unknowingly, the Lord of all.

Saint Theodosia of Caesarea Maritima is a young woman of that Palestinian city who was martyred under Maximinus Daia for a specific act of pastoral charity: she went to the prisoners condemned to death in the city's dungeon to bring them food, comfort, and prayer on the eve of their execution, and was herself arrested at the prison gate, accused of sedition for this ministry, and executed alongside those she had come to serve. The tradition honours Theodosia as the patroness of pastoral visits to the condemned, and in the Miazia dawn-context her ministry of accompanying the condemned through their final night is an image of the Church's own Lenten accompaniment of the catechumens through the darkness before the baptismal dawn.

Saint Amphianus of Caesarea Maritima is this day's most dramatic discovery: a young student of Eusebius the historian's school in Caesarea who, during a public pagan sacrifice presided over by the governor Urban, walked forward through the crowd, seized the governor's hand, and urged him publicly to abandon the worship of idols and turn to the living God. He was immediately arrested, tortured for two days with a thoroughness that Eusebius, who witnessed at least part of it, records in the Martyrs of Palestine with evident horror, and then drowned in the sea. The tradition notes that when his body was cast into the waves, the sea immediately displayed unusual turbulence, as though protesting the injustice of what it had been made to receive. Amphianus's act was not planned; he simply found himself walking forward, driven by something larger than calculation.

Saint Aedesius, the brother of Amphianus, followed his sibling's example at a short interval: where Amphianus had publicly confronted the governor of Palestine, Aedesius confronted the prefect of Egypt in Alexandria with the same apostolic directness, rebuking him publicly for his treatment of Christian slaves and prisoners. He was arrested and executed by drowning in the same manner as his brother, so that the two brothers who had both walked forward into the face of power received the same martyrdom in different cities, as though the blood of the first had called forth the courage of the second, and the family was sealed whole in the same witness. In the dawning light of Miazia, Amphianus and Aedesius are the image of the courage that is awakened by example — the courage that the approaching Resurrection is already kindling in the Lenten community. Amen.
